title: How to Talk to the Part of You That Won't Stop Worrying
researcherTopic: structural dissociation, healing the fragmented self
metaDescription: Learn to understand the part of you that won't stop worrying through the lens of Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation. Discover how to build a new relationship with your anxiety.
imageDescription: A pair of hands gently hold a luminous, golden thread, weaving it into a complex, glowing tapestry. The background is soft and warm, suggesting a process of gentle repair and integration. The overall feeling is one of hope, healing, and the quiet beauty of mending what was broken.
finalH2: From Ceaseless Vigilance to Embodied Presence
htmlContent:
Janina Fisher, a key voice in the territory of trauma recovery, spent decades mapping the terrain of the fragmented self, offering a genuine insight that the parts of us we often fight against are not enemies, but architects of survival. The theory of structural dissociation she champions suggests that the incessant worrier inside ~ the one that rehearses every possible negative outcome and scans the horizon for threats... is not a malfunction, but a highly sophisticated, protective mechanism born from necessity. Understanding this is the first step toward a different kind of internal conversation, one that moves beyond frustration and into a space of compassionate inquiry. What if that worrying part isn't the problem, but a guide trying to lead us back to a sense of wholeness? To begin this process, it's essential to first understand what complex trauma is and how it shapes our internal world.

The Unseen Architect of Your Worry

The constant hum of worry that many of us experience is not a personal failing or a sign of a broken mind; it is the diligent, tireless work of a part of the self that was created to keep us safe. In her work on structural dissociation, Janina Fisher explains that in the face of overwhelming experiences, particularly in childhood, the personality can fragment into different parts, each with its own distinct job. There are the parts that hold the trauma ~ the Emotional Parts (EPs) - and then there is the part that gets on with the business of living, the ANP. This ANP is the one that goes to work, pays the bills, and maintains relationships, all while carrying the immense burden of keeping the trauma-related parts out of conscious awareness. The worry we feel is often the sound of this ANP working overtime, a constant and exhausting vigilance that it believes is necessary for survival. It’s a guardian, a protector, a manager - and it’s been doing its job with unwavering dedication for a very long time. This part carries the cognitive and emotional load of our daily existence, and its worrying is a testament to its real commitment to our survival. It is the part that remembers the chaos and unpredictability of the past and has vowed to never let us be caught off guard again. Its methods may be exhausting, but its motives are pure. To begin to heal is to begin to recognize this part not as a source of pathology, but as a source of striking strength and resilience.

Why the Nervous System Built a Worrier-in-Chief

The Futility of Reassurance and the Language of Sensation

Dual Awareness: The Practice of Witnessing Without Fusing

Janina Fisher’s concept of “dual awareness” offers a powerful practice for working with these fragmented parts of the self. It is the ability to hold two streams of awareness simultaneously: the awareness of the present moment, and the awareness of the part’s experience. It is the capacity to be both in the here and now ~ feeling the ground beneath our feet, the air on our skin... and to acknowledge the reality of the part that is trapped in the there and then, reliving the fears and pains of the past. What emerges is not a state of being half-in, half-out, but a radical act of inclusion. It is the adult self saying to the child part, “I see you. I feel you. I know you are scared. It is the difference between being the storm and being the sky that holds the storm. This practice of dual awareness is the foundation of a new internal relationship, one that is built on presence, compassion, and the slow, steady cultivation of trust. It is a practice that we can cultivate in small moments throughout our day. When we feel a wave of anxiety, we can pause, take a breath, and acknowledge the presence of the worrying part. We can feel our feet on the floor, notice the colors in the room, and at the same time, extend a sense of kindness and curiosity to the part of us that is struggling. This is not about pushing the fear away, but about making space for it, holding it in a larger container of awareness. It is a practice of both/and, a practice of radical acceptance, a practice that slowly, over time, rewires our nervous system for safety and connection.

A New Internal Dialogue: Speaking to the Guardian

The Slow Architecture of Internal Trust

Building a relationship with the parts of ourselves that have been exiled and maligned is not a quick fix. It is the slow, patient work of building trust where there has been none. It is a process of showing up, again and again, with a compassionate and non-judgmental presence. It is about demonstrating, through our actions, that we are a safe harbor for all of our parts, even the ones that we have been taught to fear and reject. What we find here is not a process of eliminating the worrying part, but of integrating it. It is about helping it to see that it no longer needs to be on high alert, that it can relax its vigilance, that it can trust the adult self to navigate the world. This is a striking and often challenging road, one that requires a deep commitment to our own healing. As we begin to build this internal trust, we find that the constant hum of worry begins to quiet down. We find that we have more access to our creativity, our joy, our aliveness. We begin to experience a sense of wholeness that we may have never thought possible, a state that is often described in the context of Self-energy in IFS. This process is not linear. There will be days when the worrying part is loud and insistent, and days when it is quiet and distant. The key is to remain steady in our commitment to this new relationship, to continue to show up with kindness and curiosity, to continue to offer the safety and reassurance that was so painfully absent in the past. This is the slow, steady work of re-weaving the fabric of the self, of creating a new internal pattern that is strong, resilient, and whole.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link).

From Ceaseless Vigilance to Embodied Presence

The ultimate goal of this work is not to get rid of the worrying part, but to help it to transform. It is to help it to see that its energy, its intelligence, its vigilance can be used in the service of a different kind of life - a life that is not defined by fear, but by presence. As we build a relationship with this part, as we offer it the safety and compassion that it has been so desperately craving, it begins to relax. Its ceaseless vigilance gives way to a more embodied and discerning awareness. It learns to differentiate between real threats and the echoes of past trauma. It begins to trust that the adult self is capable of handling the challenges of life. But with practice, we can learn to meet these moments with a sense of curiosity and compassion, rather than with fear and frustration. We can learn to see them as opportunities to deepen our relationship with this part of ourselves, to offer it more of the safety and reassurance that it needs. This is the process from a life of ceaseless vigilance to a life of embodied presence, a life in which we are no longer at war with ourselves, but are instead a compassionate and integrated whole. This reworking is not a destination, but a process, a dance between the past and the present, between the parts and the whole. It is a road of coming home to ourselves, of reclaiming the parts of us that have been lost in the shadows, of finally, finally, learning to befriend the beautiful, broken, and resilient beings that we are.

So what are you going to do about it? Not tomorrow. Right now. The body is waiting for your answer.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link).

Frequently Asked Questions

Is the worrying part the same as my anxiety?

While they are deeply interconnected, it can be helpful to see the worrying part as the *source* of the anxiety. The part is the entity, the sub-personality, that is generating the anxious thoughts and feelings as a protective strategy. By relating to the part itself, we can get to the root of the anxiety, rather than just trying to manage the symptoms.

Will this practice get rid of my worrying part forever?

The goal is not to eliminate any part of yourself, but to integrate it. The worrying part has valuable qualities ~ it is vigilant, strategic, and deeply committed to your survival. It’s a job change, not a termination.

Think about that for a second.

For practical self-compassion exercises, a popular workbook is The Self-Compassion Workbook (paid link).

What if I can't feel or hear any parts?

What we find here is very common. For many, the concept of "parts" is purely intellectual at first. When you feel that familiar knot of worry in your stomach, you can simply acknowledge, "Ah, the worrying part is here." You don't need to have a full-blown conversation. The simple act of acknowledging its presence with curiosity is the beginning of the relationship.

What becomes possible when the energy we spend fighting ourselves is finally liberated for the work of living?

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link).

The question is not whether you are ready. It is whether you are willing.
